id: Consulting - GTM Transformation - Lifesciences Technology, Global
title: Accelerated Growth Through Global Go-to-Market Transformation
hook: Rebuilding a $1.3B life sciences operating model to reverse attrition and double revenue growth
challenges: Operating model misaligned after fourteen bolt-on acquisitions with minimal integration
No clear enterprise-wide strategy and significant underutilization of technology platforms
Channel and role conflict suppressing growth while leadership capability gaps limited agility
type_of_reinvention: Global GTM transformation, technology and platform enablement
ambition: A $1.3B life sciences and technology business had grown rapidly through fourteen bolt-on acquisitions, but the cumulative weight of redundant operations had pushed its cost-to-serve out of line with the market. NPS was declining as customer attrition mounted, employee attrition was rising in parallel, and pressures on productivity, efficiency, and cost were converging on a single conclusion: the operating model needed to be reinvented from the ground up.
challenge: Beneath the surface metrics sat a deeper set of structural problems. There was no clear enterprise-wide strategy or operating alignment; acquisitions had been onboarded with minimal and inconsistent integration; investment in automation, platforms, and e-business channels was being underutilized; channel and role conflict was suppressing growth; and leadership capability gaps were limiting the organization's ability to execute at the pace the market demanded.
solutions: We co-created a Single Point of Truth with leadership across geographies, business units, and functions, anchoring the transformation in a shared, owned vision. From there we reimagined the organization customer-backward — redesigning segmentation, channels, sales process, workflows, and organization design around how customers actually buy and consume — and rebuilt ways of working, roles, capabilities, and talent alignment to match the new operating model. Change was enabled at speed and activated enterprise-wide, led from the middle by high-potential ambassadors, sponsored by executives, and engaged across functions, geographies, and customers.
benefits: Doubled revenue growth within two years and accelerated profitability through e-business and low-cost channels
Expanded capacity and new-product adoption through non-traditional, low-cost, and specialist routes to market
Sales attrition fell by 6%, 75% of staff lifted their performance, and employee engagement strengthened across the organization

id: Consulting - R&D Transformation - Tech Software, Global
title: Accelerating Innovation and Solutions Portfolio Focus Through R&D Transformation
hook: Transforming a global software developer into an IP-centric solutions provider through R&D reinvention
challenges: Slow to innovate, with capability and resources misaligned to market demand
Cost structure locked to a legacy R&D model
Product portfolio focused on features rather than customer solutions
type_of_reinvention: Product to Solutions, R&D Reinvention, Offshoring, M&A
ambition: Serve as an innovation and growth engine for the broader organization. The client sought to shift from being a developer of software to a provider of integrated customer solutions, transforming its organization, functions, processes, and skills to develop, partner, distribute, sell, and service those solutions.
challenge: The organization was slow to innovate, with capabilities and resources misaligned to its new direction. Its cost structure was tied to a legacy R&D model, and the product portfolio remained focused on product features rather than customer solutions.
solutions: At the heart of the engagement was an R&D transformation that established an IP-centric software core. We established an Offshore Partnership (OSP) and transitioned offshore teams to scale capacity, while pursuing strategic acquisition and integration of technology and capability. Around this we restructured organisational design, ways of working, roles, and skills, and ran the change management needed to establish and sustain the organisational and behavioral shifts.
benefits: Transformed R&D into a premier IP-centric software core, accelerating innovation and product throughput
Transitioned two-thirds of the software development workforce to an OSP provider, creating a 66% increase in capacity
Decreased expenses by 5% and reduced attrition by 10%
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] The decision to transform R&D was also a decision about what the business was not. Invensys Wonderware had world-class development talent — the constraint was structural: a cost model and capability mix built for producing features, not designing integrated solutions. The offshore partnership was not primarily a cost play; it was a capacity unlock that freed the IP core to stop doing commodity development work and start thinking at the solutions layer. The acquisitions served the same logic — they were the fastest route to capability that the organic model could not produce in time. The client insight is that a product-to-solutions transformation requires changing the architecture of R&D, not just its priorities: until the structure changes, the old gravity wins.

id: Consulting - Cultural Transformation - Professional Services, Greater China
title: Cultural Reinvention to Halt Attrition and Reset a Hierarchical, Change-Fatigued Workforce
hook: Halving second-year associate turnover and rebuilding a culture of accountability across 8,500 staff
challenges: Second-year associate churn at up to 70% in affected regions
Employee engagement declining approximately 3% year-on-year
Previous transformation efforts had failed, compounding change fatigue across the division
type_of_reinvention: Cultural transformation, behavior change
ambition: PwC's Greater China Assurance division was growing at ~20% per year, but that success was being undermined by crippling employee turnover. Second-year associates, the firm's most productive cohort, were leaving at rates as high as 70% in some regions. Engagement had been declining ~3% year-on-year, institutional knowledge was bleeding out, and the operational and reputational cost of the churn threatened the division's capacity for sustained growth.
challenge: The attrition was a symptom of an authoritarian, hierarchical operating culture in which senior leaders set a use-and-discard tone that middle managers replicated downward. Four interlocking root causes surfaced: a lack of accountability for leadership behavior at the top; change fatigue from previous failed initiatives that had bred cynicism toward any new program; the objectification of employees as interchangeable resources rather than developed talent; and unaddressed workload pressures that were normalising the dysfunction under the banner of "too busy to change."
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] Mindful of the accumulated change fatigue in the organization, we deliberately integrated the transformation with existing initiatives rather than positioning it as yet another new program. At a leadership level, we held Partners to explicit behavioral standards through performance improvement plans with direct bonus consequences — a clear signal that leadership behavior was no longer a matter of personal style but of organisational accountability. To build momentum across the broader organization, we ran a Day of Change and a series of cultural forums, equipping middle managers to serve as credible ambassadors for the transformation. Ambassadors were required to publish their personal pledges to change on PwC's internal network — making accountability visible, human, and sustainable.
activities: Held partners to explicit behavioral standards through Performance Improvement Plans with bonus consequences, signaling that tolerance for poor leadership behavior was no longer acceptable
Ran a Day of Change event and ongoing cultural forums, equipping middle managers as ambassadors, each publishing a personal commitment to change on PwC's internal network
Launched a Breakthrough Blog to sustain transparent dialog between leadership and staff throughout the program
Integrated the program with existing initiatives, including diversity and the PwC Way, to defuse change fatigue and build on established momentum
Monitored behavioral expectations with warnings and sanctions for non-compliance, and supportive training and mentorship for those willing to adapt
benefits: Second-year associate churn fell from up to 70% to 20–25% in affected regions within twelve months, sharply reducing recruitment and training costs and restoring continuity to client engagements
PwC's People Engagement Index, which had been declining year-on-year before the program, improved by 20%
Client service stability strengthened alongside team cohesion, and PwC reinforced its market reputation as a progressive, people-centric firm
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] The root cause of unsustainable turnover in a high-performing professional services firm is rarely workload alone — it is leadership behavior that goes unaccountable. The decisive move in this engagement was not a culture program: it was tying Partner behavior to bonus consequences, which turned a soft cultural expectation into a hard commercial accountability. When accountability became structural rather than aspirational, the culture followed.

id: Consulting - Behaviour Change - Professional Services, Hong Kong
title: De-toxifying the Workplace and Reversing Churn for PwC in Hong Kong
hook: Six months to shift a cut-throat, dictatorial culture to collaborative and supportive — and reverse 84% SA2 churn
challenges: Senior associate (SA2) churn rising to 84% in Hong Kong — employees at peak productivity leaving in unprecedented numbers
Employee engagement falling 14-16% year-on-year, with no prior transformation effort achieving sustained behavioral change
Behavioral failures at every level: disengaged partners, dictatorial managers, disenchanted associates feeling disrespected
type_of_reinvention: Behavior change, cultural reinvention, leadership accountability
ambition: PwC's regional offices in Hong Kong had a worsening problem: a culture that had become progressively toxic was beginning to threaten the business. The most visible symptom was an SA2 (senior associate, two-year tenure) churn rate of 84% — employees at the point of peak productivity were leaving at an astounding rate. Employee engagement had fallen 14-16% year-on-year, and a previous transformation attempt had delivered technology change but failed to produce the broader behavioral shift the organization needed. Whitewater was engaged to fix the root cause: the culture, from leadership down.
challenge: Root cause analysis across every level of the organization revealed a consistent pattern. Partners and directors were disengaged from key operational areas, citing time pressure as justification for non-involvement. Senior managers and managers had adopted dictatorial, coercive attitudes toward subordinates. Associates and senior associates felt disrespected and disenchanted with their working environment. The high-churn, low-engagement outcomes were not random — they were the direct product of leadership behavior that had never been held accountable. A failed prior transformation attempt had also created scepticism about whether meaningful change was possible.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] A three-pronged Day of Change (DoC) was implemented: unacceptable behaviors were identified, documented, and publicly named; new technology was deployed to monitor cultural change; and clear warnings and sanctions were introduced for those who reverted to toxic behaviors. The DoC was designed to demarcate the past from the future — with the leadership publicly facing up to the ingrained bad practices that had taken hold, and presenting a clear, credible vision of what a reinvented organization looks like. By communicating this unambiguously to the whole firm, a culture of accountability was installed at every level. Partners became more engaged, managers adopted more supportive and collaborative behaviors, and associates felt valued — a shift evidenced through staff surveys conducted after the engagement closed.
benefits: SA2 churn reversed from 84% in Hong Kong; engagement and retention significantly improved across all levels
Culture shifted from cut-throat and dictatorial to transparent, collaborative, and supportive across the firm
Improved client perceptions: responsiveness and differentiation in audit services increased as the internal culture stabilized
Leadership accountability installed at every level through the Day of Change framework and ongoing monitoring
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] The insight that drove this engagement was also the most uncomfortable one: the churn was not caused by workload. It was caused by unchecked leadership behavior cascading downward through every tier of the firm. Fixing the culture required naming the behaviors publicly, making accountability visible at the leadership level, and giving the organization a credible break point — the Day of Change — rather than another soft initiative. Six months was enough because the diagnosis was precise and the intervention addressed the actual root cause.

id: Consulting - Technology & Operating Model - Air Cargo, Global
title: Technology & Operating Model Reinvention for Global Air Cargo Carrier
hook: Turning a three-year operating system overhaul into sustained behavior change across 4,000 staff in 100+ locations
challenges: Operating system replacement spanning 4,000 staff across 100+ global locations with no shared change vision
Stakeholders at every level held conflicting views on the existing system, the new one, and their own readiness to change
Risk of technical implementation succeeding while the organization resisted, fragmented, or failed to adopt
type_of_reinvention: Technology implementation, operating model reinvention, and culture change
ambition: One of the world’s leading air cargo carriers faced one of the most complex transformation challenges an airline can undertake: replacing its entire operating system and related applications with a new, integrated technology platform. The project was expected to span more than three years, touching approximately 4,000 staff across headquarters in Hong Kong and over 100 locations globally. For an organization of this scale and operational precision, a technology change of this magnitude was not simply an IT implementation — it was a fundamental shift in how people worked, communicated, and made decisions every day.
challenge: The operating system change was unlike anything most affected employees had experienced before. The complexity was not just technical — it was human. Stakeholders across every level of the organization held different views on the challenges with the existing system, different expectations of what the new one would deliver, and different degrees of readiness to change. Without a shared vision and a structured approach to engagement, the risk was not that the technology would fail, but that the organization around it would resist, fragment, or simply not adopt it at the pace the business required.
solutions: We began by working closely with senior leadership to develop a shared change vision and co-created change journey. From there, we conducted a comprehensive stakeholder analysis, mapping the impact of the system change across the organization and assessing change readiness. We co-designed the change management framework and execution approach with the client project team directly — ensuring full internal ownership from the outset. Central to this was the establishment of a Change Management Center embedded within each impacted department and region, creating a permanent forum for stakeholders to raise concerns, surface practical issues, and contribute to solutions. We then worked alongside the client to design and roll out tailored engagement programs across both headquarters and regional outports.
benefits: Successful adoption of new operating model
Measurable shift in employee behavior, with client leadership reporting increased self-motivation, self-initiated programs, and a stronger orientation toward practical, outcome-driven problem solving
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] A technology replacement of this scale is always, at its core, a human transformation. The lesson from this engagement was that adoption risk lives in the gap between technical readiness and human readiness — and that closing that gap required permanent infrastructure embedded inside the organization, not a parallel change team running alongside it. The Change Management Center model turned stakeholder friction into structured input, and made the organization itself the engine of adoption.

id: Consulting - Change & Operating Model - Supply Chain Manufacturing, Global

Technology transformation investment failing to deliver — operating model never redesigned to support it

Regions were disconnected, leadership capabilities and role expectations were misaligned, and governance was reinforcing unproductive behavior. solutions: [DRAFT — for review] Partnering with the CEO, Board, and Executive Team, we co-created an end-to-end redesign of the global procurement organization — covering targets, incentives, leadership structure, organisational design, governance, and interfaces. New operating model that unlocked global synergies and increased agility, positioning Procurement to capture more than €500 million in savings while embedding new ways of working

id: Consulting - Post-Acquisition Turnaround - Automotive Manufacturing, Global
title: Post-Acquisition Turnaround of a Global Automotive Parts Manufacturer
hook: Delivering $68M in savings and reversing a fifteen-year decline at a global automotive parts manufacturer — in 12 months
challenges: Fifteen years of decline: minimal investment, outdated technology, $40M annual losses across nine global sites
Weak financial controls, no Lean capability, and a fragmented Executive Leadership Team unable to act as one
Low morale and loss of workforce confidence across regional sites with no shared direction
type_of_reinvention: Post-acquisition restructure, operational turnaround, cultural change
ambition: A multinational automotive components manufacturer had been in decline for over fifteen years when it was acquired. Minimal investment since 2009 and outdated technology had steadily eroded its competitive position, leaving a strained workforce spread across nine global manufacturing sites and annual losses of $40 million with no clear path to recovery. The incoming owner needed to stop the bleeding, stabilize the business, and lay the foundation for a viable, future-oriented organization — at pace.
challenge: Challenges ran across every dimension of the business simultaneously. Financial controls were weak and $68 million in savings were urgently required, yet the organization lacked the discipline or visibility to know where to begin. The production footprint was high-cost and outdated, with no Lean methodology in place to drive efficiency. The Executive Leadership Team had been reassembled after years apart and lacked the cohesion needed to drive coordinated change. And without a permanent CFO, the finance team was operating without the controls or reporting capability needed to manage a turnaround. Culturally, regional inconsistency across nine sites had bred low morale and a workforce that had long since lost confidence in the organization's direction.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] We began with a comprehensive diagnostic: 60+ manager interviews across key global sites mapped the operational, financial, and cultural condition of the business and set the baseline for the reinvention. These findings informed a series of SPoT (Single Point of Truth) and master-plan workshops that brought the Executive Leadership Team into genuine alignment behind a single, unified strategy — for many of them, for the first time. To stabilize the immediate financial situation, we appointed an interim finance consultant who restructured the finance team, implemented proper controls and reporting, and prepared the ground for the incoming permanent CFO. Lean transformation programs were then deployed at the highest-cost manufacturing sites — particularly in North America — with production line shutdowns, relocations, and retraining programs designed to right-size the footprint and drive efficiency. We implemented Team Assurance (TA), a digital accountability platform, to give the CEO and ELT real-time visibility across all workstreams; bi-weekly CEO-led reviews enforced delivery against targets. Finally, SPoT workshops and behavioral programs were decentralized to plant level, empowering site leaders to own the reinvention within their own operations and build the capability to sustain results long after the engagement ended.
benefits: $68M in cost savings delivered through plant shutdowns, relocations, and Lean efficiencies — ending the years of annual losses
Productivity up to 20% higher at key manufacturing sites; unit costs reduced and production aligned to best-in-class efficiency standards
Finance team restructured and controls implemented; interim CFO laid the groundwork so the incoming permanent CFO could act immediately on arrival
ELT brought into genuine alignment for the first time; bi-weekly accountability reviews via Team Assurance created a disciplined, results-focused leadership team
Culture shifted from low morale and regional inconsistency toward proactive, cross-functional ownership of the turnaround across nine global sites
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] A fifteen-year decline cannot be reversed by a single lever. What made this turnaround work was that the intervention operated simultaneously across three dimensions — structural, operational, and behavioral. SPoT workshops brought the ELT into genuine alignment, some of them for the first time. Lean built real operating discipline across the production footprint. And decentralising ownership to plant level created the accountability infrastructure that would hold the gains once the engagement ended. The CFO summed it up: "Before I began, the company finances were in a bit of a mess. Whitewater helped lay the foundations... they instilled some financial rigour, so that I could really hit the ground running once I was in post."

id: Consulting - Pre-Acquisition Assessment - Private Equity, Global
title: Supporting a Private Equity Firm to Assess the Transformation Readiness of a Potential Acquisition
headcount: [AI — verify] mid-eight figure USD annual losses cited in source; total employee count not stated
hook: From opaque asset to confident acquisition: ~$70M in identified savings and a post-acquisition roadmap before the ink was dry
challenges: Opaque asset condition: target had undergone prior acquisitions, infrastructure underinvested for years, nine manufacturing sites with unknown operational state
Target company haemorrhaging mid-eight figures USD annually; no CFO, weak controls, no comprehensive financial strategy
Disjointed leadership team reassembled after years apart; key managers ill-equipped to lead the required transformation
Multi-region operations with fragmented systems, different business practices, and resistance to change across the board
type_of_reinvention: Pre-acquisition assessment, operational diagnostics, post-acquisition stabilization support
ambition: A world-renowned private equity firm was considering the acquisition of a multinational automotive components manufacturer — a complex, multi-site business that had been in decline for years. Before proceeding with the investment, the PE firm needed genuine clarity on the operational, financial, and cultural state of the target: what they were buying, what it would take to fix it, and whether the leadership team was capable of executing the required transformation. Whitewater was engaged to conduct that comprehensive assessment and, upon acquisition, to support the post-acquisition phase by implementing its own recommendations.
challenge: The PE firm faced significant uncertainties. The target had undergone previous acquisitions with a history of underinvestment — the condition of its nine global manufacturing sites was simply not clear. Financially, the company was haemorrhaging a mid-eight figure USD sum annually, with limited visibility into the structure, weak controls, and no permanent CFO. Leadership lacked cohesion, having been reassembled after years apart, and key managers were ill-equipped for the transformation ahead. Across multiple regions, each with its own business practices, fragmented systems and entrenched resistance to change created additional acquisition risk that could not be dismissed.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] We initiated a global assessment spanning 50+ senior manager interviews across five countries, analysing key financial, operational, and organisational metrics using proprietary diagnostic tools to benchmark the target against industry standards. Detailed capability profiles were built for senior managers, mapping them against the roles the post-acquisition business would require — revealing significant skill gaps, particularly in finance. To address the immediate leadership vacuum, we provided interim CFO services: the new appointment created financial reporting transparency, implemented forecasting processes, and laid the groundwork for the permanent CFO to act on arrival. SPoT (Single Point of Truth) workshops were used to engage the executive leadership team around immediate and long-term strategic goals — enabling the PE firm to directly evaluate management readiness. Lean manufacturing audits across nine production sites identified inefficiencies in cost, output, and process, producing specific recommendations including plant closures, Lean implementation, and workforce retraining. benefits: ~$70M in annual savings identified and initiated through plant closures, Lean transformations, and cost management interventions
Interim CFO installed immediately to fix financial reporting, create transparency, and prepare for the permanent appointment
Leadership team evaluated for transformation readiness; reorganized structure with defined accountabilities positioned management to drive sustainable change
Productivity rose 19.5% across key manufacturing sites post-acquisition; operational costs significantly reduced, particularly in North America and Europe
PE firm empowered to proceed with the acquisition with confidence and a fully structured post-acquisition roadmap
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] Pre-acquisition due diligence for a distressed asset is not just financial modeling — it is a leadership and culture assessment. The most consequential finding in this engagement was not the $70M savings opportunity (which was expected) but the readiness assessment of the management team: who could lead the transformation and who could not. Installing an interim CFO during the assessment phase — before the acquisition closed — meant the PE firm arrived into a business with functioning financial controls rather than inheriting the chaos. The pre-acquisition support extended well beyond analysis into active intervention, and that head start determined the pace of the post-acquisition turnaround.

id: Consulting - Post-Merger Integration & Turnaround - FMCG, Western Europe
title: Returned a Declining FMCG Brand from Value Erosion to Market Leadership
headcount: [AI — verify] ~5,000 — stated in source as the workforce in global operations covered by the reinvention
hook: Fastest-growing company in their European category within two years — starting from a business that had been losing market share for years
challenges: Company losing >1% market share per year with insipid profits over multiple years
Leadership entrenched in a maintenance mindset; key personnel placed to manage decline, not execute an aggressive turnaround
Highly unionized factories limiting production flexibility; silos embedded both vertically and horizontally across the organization
General culture of acceptance that mediocrity was tolerable; widespread scepticism about change initiatives
type_of_reinvention: Post-merger integration, turnaround, cultural transformation, operational reinvention
ambition: A high-profile European cooperative acquired several fast-moving consumer goods global brands from a multinational competitor with an ambitious mandate: not just to stop the steady decline, but to achieve 20% annual growth. The business had been losing market share for years and had delivered insipid profits for as long as anyone could remember. The vision was to restore profitability and market share while building a culture of continuous growth, innovation, agility, and entrepreneurial energy across 5,000 staff in global operations.
challenge: The business was in dire straits at the time of acquisition. It was losing more than 1% market share per year and had delivered insipid profits for many years. Leadership had been put in place to manage a steady decline and were entrenched in a maintenance mindset — not equipped for an aggressive turnaround. The company's ways of working were siloed both vertically across departments and horizontally across organisational levels, killing communication, collaboration, and innovation. The highly unionized factory environment severely limited production flexibility. And there was a pervasive culture of acceptance: losing money and market share was tolerable, change plans were viewed with scepticism, and most managers had resigned themselves to a static future.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] The first step was to build a unified leadership vision — a co-created "Single-Point-of-Truth" (SPoT) projecting the organization three years forward as a high-growth, fast-moving business. Every leader was given clear accountability for a specific area of the reinvention, with a detailed milestone plan and bi-weekly check-ins to enforce on-time delivery. Leadership assessments identified those capable of adapting to the new environment and those who would struggle — informing the composition of the new post-acquisition leadership team. The cultural shift was engineered through a company-wide "Day of Change" event, led by junior and middle managers using frontline language, designed to break with the past and unite the organization under the new vision. Approximately 100 high-potential employees (Ambassadors) were activated across all business units to bridge the gap between executive strategy and frontline operations — breaking silos by translating strategic vision into daily actions and fostering ownership at every level. Factory operations were revitalized through empowerment-driven process improvement: sales teams eliminated obsolete tasks, union agreements were renegotiated for greater flexibility, and a bottom-up improvement culture replaced the top-down directive model. Small but symbolic morale initiatives — including reinstating annual factory picnics and long-service recognitions — restored a sense of pride and loyalty that directly translated into engagement and alignment with the growth goals.
benefits: 20% annual growth achieved within 2 years — against a 3-year plan — making the company the fastest-growing in its category in Europe
Manufacturing productivity >10% improvement in 24 months; factories that had been bound by rigid union agreements became flexible and capable of scaling
2x improvement ideas submitted by end of year one as bottom-up entrepreneurial culture took hold
18% waste reduction in year one through supply chain reinvention and operational empowerment
4 key union agreements renegotiated for greater production flexibility
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] The reinvention's speed of impact was determined by where the energy came from. By leading the Day of Change through junior and middle managers — not executives — and activating ~100 Ambassadors as bridges between strategy and frontline, the organization stopped waiting for top-down permission to change. The CEO described it simply: people are better at explaining than forecasting. The ambition was co-created, not handed down. As a result, 20% growth was achieved not in three years as planned but in two — because the organization did not need external management of its own transformation. It owned it.

id: Consulting - AI Analytics & Performance Reinvention - QSR Franchise, Japan
title: AI-Driven Store Economics Reinvention for a Major QSR Franchise Network
hook: AI correlation analysis of 767 stores proves that labor discipline (not location, scale, or aggregators) is the single lever that makes or breaks each QSR franchise
challenges: Store profitability data resided in two disconnected systems, operational and financial, that had never been joined at the store level
Management was operating on untested assumptions about the drivers of store performance, including location, scale, and aggregator mix
Most franchise stores were operationally cash-positive but weighed down by loan obligations to the Client; the underlying problem had been misread as operational
type_of_reinvention: AI-based analytics, performance improvement program, franchise operations reinvention
duration: Stage 1 (analytics evidence base) delivered June 2026; Stage 2 (PIP): implementation and monthly reporting by store, 26-week program
ambition: The Client with a workforce of 15,000 in Japan wanted to stop store unprofitability for 50% of its outlets. They needed independent AI-based analytics to identify correlations to turn the company around. The ambition was to compile many (>80) data sources that had never been compiled before to create evidence-based insights. Evidence-based approach not received wisdom, then take the insights to create a store-by-store customized Performance Improvement Process (PIP) across the 767-store network. The Operations Team had reported an inability to “squeeze” any further performance improvement from the stores after 4 years of losses with minimal improvements in performance.
challenge:
The Client’s data sat in two disconnected systems as well as several unconnected locations, requiring significant cleaning before any data could be trusted.

The organization had accumulated a set of widely held beliefs about what drove store performance: location, scale, manager churn, supply-chain costs and manager competencies; these assumptions were not supported by the data, i.e. minimal correlation.
solutions:
Whitewater analyzed all the data store-by-store for two sets of stores: Company-owned and Franchise-owned. Running every controllable store variable through an AI-driven correlation analysis, with no preconceived view of what levers were significantly impactful. Strongly profitable were compared to struggling stores.

The analysis produced 7 performance myths (assumptions held widely but contradicted by minimal correlation) and 2 action insights focused on labor discipline and sales volume as the two dominant levers of profitability.

From these analytics, Whitewater was able to work with the Client’s executives to develop a 26-week, four-sprint Performance Improvement Process (PIP) that enabled the area operations teams to drive significant improvement in profitability in 3 x 2 month sprints covering all 767 stores. Prime focus was on 102 priority franchise stores running labor above the 30% danger threshold as the primary intervention cohort.
benefits: Identified labor cost % and sales volume as the two dominant levers of store profitability, each carrying 9x the influence of food cost and 20x store age

Debunked 7 performance myths, including the assumptions that location, multi-store ownership, and delivery aggregators drive profitability, redirecting management attention to the two levers that move cash
Isolated 102 priority franchise stores running labor above the 30% danger threshold, with significant average recoverable cash per month
Proved that ¥5,000 saved on labor delivers the same cash impact as ¥38,000 in extra sales, reframing the operational priority for every store manager in the network
Designed a 26-week, four-sprint PIP framework covering all 767 store managers, with tailored interventions per competency/profitability quadrant and measurable EBITDA targets per sprint
client_insight:
The Client had never placed their fundamental business model under the microscope. Clarity of focus on 2 areas rather than 20 areas helped turn the entire company around.

Another revealing insight was what the data ruled out. Nearly every assumption the Client’s Operations Team used to explain weak stores were proved erroneous (e.g. bad locations, franchisees running too many stores, managers paying themselves too much, lower labor costs leading to poor NPS scores). The implication is direct: weak stores are not structurally doomed by their circumstances, all are operationally fixable. The simple and robust mechanism of clear data-led PIP targets with monthly reporting drove the turnaround.

id: 99 Consulting - Culture Review and Training Design - Financial Regulator, Hong Kong
title: Culture Review and Training Design for a Major Financial Regulator in Hong Kong

source_file: 99 Consulting - Culture Review and Training Design - Financial Regulator, Hong Kong.pptx
hook: Building the shared cultural foundation a Hong Kong regulator needed to make improvement stick
challenges: Years of overlapping improvement initiatives had left the organization without a shared, honest account of its own culture to build from
Senior management had not aligned on either what the current culture was or what the desired culture required — making any cascading program premature
Across 500+ employees, perceptions of behavior, communication, and openness to change varied significantly, with no baseline to anchor them
ambition: A statutory regulatory body in Hong Kong with more than 500 employees had spent several years implementing improvement initiatives, but without first establishing a shared, honest understanding of its own culture. The organization recognized that it could not define a compelling future direction — or cascade it credibly — until leadership had reached genuine consensus on where the culture stood and what behaviors needed to change. The ambition was to ground the transformation in diagnosis before prescription.
challenge: The organization had accumulated multiple improvement initiatives over recent years, but their cumulative effect had been to produce change fatigue and a diffuse sense of direction rather than momentum. Across the organization, employees held different views on current behaviors, communication norms, engagement levels, and the culture's openness to change. At senior management level, consensus had not been built on either what the current culture genuinely looked like or what the desired culture would require — making any attempt to cascade a new culture premature.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] We began with a comprehensive assessment of the current corporate culture, examining employee behavior, internal communication patterns, engagement levels, and perception of change across the organization. The findings were used to facilitate a structured consensus-building process at senior management level, producing a shared and honest account of the current culture and a clear articulation of the specific behaviors to be changed and the behaviors to be sustained. From this aligned foundation, we worked with the organization to develop and codify a clear vision, mission, and values. We then designed a culture cascading program and delivery infrastructure to bring the new culture to middle management level, equipping managers with the tools and supportive mechanisms to embed new behaviors beyond the engagement.
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] Regulatory bodies are often change-resistant not because their people oppose improvement, but because their culture of risk-aversion makes it difficult to name honestly what is not working. In this engagement, the most important intervention was creating the conditions for senior leadership to reach genuine consensus on the current cultural reality — not the aspirational version of it. Once that honest baseline existed, the cascading program had something credible to cascade. The vision, mission, and values that followed were not aspirational wallpaper; they were grounded in a shared diagnosis. That grounding is what made the cascade land.
type_of_reinvention: Culture review and training design

ambition: As a strategic move to improve operating efficiency, a multinational company made the decision to terminate its joint venture operation in Shanghai and establish a new wholly-owned foreign enterprise in a new location. The JV closure required the simultaneous negotiation of a termination agreement with the JV partner, the physical transfer of all assets, the termination of four lease contracts, and the harmonious downsizing of 300 employees — all on a compressed timeline and in a regulatory environment the client was not equipped to navigate alone.
challenge: The client faced four overlapping operational tracks simultaneously: reaching a cooperative termination settlement with the JV partner; coordinating the legal and logistical transfer of 150 containers of assets, 300 individual customer orders, and all associated documentation; managing the shutdown of four lease contracts; and standing down 300 employees in a way that avoided disruption, government friction, or industrial action. With limited internal experience managing closures of this complexity in China, the client had no practical basis for executing all four tracks in parallel — or for preventing any one of them from derailing the others.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] We took on an active project management and negotiation support role across all four tracks of the JV closure. Working alongside the client's negotiating team, we developed a comprehensive termination checklist, reviewed and commented on lease contracts and the assets transfer and payment schedule, and monitored the implementation of agreed conditions against the termination agreement. We bridged communication between both parties — internally and externally — to prevent misunderstanding from derailing the timeline. On-site, we facilitated daily asset transfer checks against original delivery documents and certificates, established a systematic tracking process for finished goods, WIP, and other assets, and managed communication with employees, government officials, and other stakeholders throughout the closure. On the workforce track, we reviewed and oversaw the finalization of the retrenchment package and managed on-site communication with employees to ensure the downsizing was handled with full transparency and government support.
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] JV terminations in China fail quietly — not in the negotiation room, but in the implementation. The risks are diffuse: a missed document in the asset transfer, a miscommunication with a government official, a retrenchment package that the workforce perceives as unfair. In this engagement, the client's core problem was not legal or strategic — it was operational: four complex, interdependent tracks running simultaneously in a regulatory environment they did not fully understand. The value we delivered was not in the advice but in being physically on-site, managing the detail, and ensuring that nothing slipped between the tracks when the cost of slippage was disruption.

type_of_reinvention: Factory relocation, workforce planning and transition
hook: Building the workforce plan for a 2,000-person factory relocation — from opening day to full ramp-up five years later
challenges: Relocating 2,000 employees from an existing factory while simultaneously designing the workforce model for a 3,500-person ramp-up over the following five years
Every employment event had to be precisely timed against technical project milestones — equipment relocation, commissioning, and staged production start — with no room for slip
Community relations required significant local recruitment for rare technical skills, with extended lead times that had to be built into the plan without compromising on-time onboarding
ambition: The energy division of a large European industrial conglomerate had acquired a Chinese state-owned enterprise — a leading local boiler manufacturer — and successfully completed its integration. The next phase was more ambitious: upgrading the manufacturing facilities to build more, cheaper, and better boilers at scale for the Asian market. The upgrade required relocating 2,000 employees from the existing factory and building a workforce plan that would carry the new site from opening to a full 3,500-person operation over the following five years.
challenge: The complexity of the relocation was not in moving the equipment — it was in synchronising the workforce with it. Every employment event needed to be planned against the key technical milestones: equipment relocation, commissioning, start of production, and staged ramp-up. The planning had to be flexible enough to absorb changing circumstances without losing alignment across a ten-year employment horizon. Community relations further constrained the approach: a significant proportion of employees needed to be recruited locally, and rare technical skills — which required longer lead times to source — had to be onboarded precisely on time or the production schedule would slip.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] We designed and delivered a Staffing Planning Program (SPP) — a flexible, comprehensive planning model that covered every employment event across the relocation and subsequent ramp-up phases. The SPP linked all hiring, assessment, placement, onboarding, and training activities to the key technical project milestones — equipment relocation, commissioning, and staged production ramp-up — and projected the full workforce requirements through to 3,500 employees over five years. To ensure the plan could flex without breaking, we built "what if" scenario capability directly into the model, allowing the client to test the employment impact of changing circumstances in real time. On the ground, we managed sourcing and recruitment for hard-to-find technical skills with extended lead times built in for rare profiles, and coordinated with educational institutions to establish training programs for the longer-term workforce pipeline.
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] A factory relocation is a logistics program that is also a human program — and the two can only be synchronized by treating the workforce plan as a live project management tool, not a headcount spreadsheet. In this engagement, the SPP was not a deliverable that sat on a shelf after handover; it was a running tool the client could adapt on an ongoing basis, with "what if" flexibility built in to absorb the changes that inevitably come in a ten-year build. The distinctive discipline was designing backward from the technical milestones rather than forward from an HR plan — which meant the workforce was always keyed to the production reality, not to an assumption about it.

activities: PwC Personnel conducted a mapping analysis through structured interviews with selected staff.  Special care was taken to ensure representation across regions, levels, and “source” organization.

Identified specific training gaps and commercial effectiveness weaknesses at store and channel level, with findings structured by region and sales channel for immediate use

hook: Rescuing a stalled post-acquisition integration by surfacing the cultural blind spots leadership could not see
challenges: An initial integration attempt had already failed — employees on both sides openly described the combined culture as "oil and water"
Leadership held significant blind spots about disengagement, entrenched silos, and us-and-them thinking at junior sales levels across the combined organization
No shared vision, communication strategy, or transformation plan existed to align the combined sales organization behind a single direction
ambition: A U.S. company had acquired a division from its key competitor in China, inheriting a fundamentally different culture and leadership style. An initial integration attempt had already produced evidence of failure — disengagement, entrenched silos, and persistent us-and-them thinking across regions and channels. The client needed an independent assessment that would cut through the assumption and surface what was actually happening across the combined salesforce, and a week-by-week transformation plan to act on the findings immediately.
challenge: The two organizations had never operated as one, and the initial integration effort had made the cultural divide more visible rather than less. Across the sales organization, employees in both source companies held deeply different views on culture, leadership effectiveness, and communication — with the friction most acute at junior sales levels where management visibility was lowest. Leadership's blind spots meant they could not see what was breaking down or why. Without a credible, independent diagnostic, there was no basis for designing an integration approach that could actually work.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] We conducted a structured mapping analysis through interviews with selected staff across the combined salesforce, taking care to ensure representation from all regions, management levels, and both source organizations. Alongside cultural mapping, we assessed corporate culture, leadership competencies, staff attitudes and expectations, and commercial effectiveness — using calibrated scoring with qualitative commentary to give the findings both precision and texture. The analysis surfaced significant management blind spots: abrasive leaders, internally competitive behavior, training gaps, and poor merchandising visibility at store level — structured by region and sales channel so findings were immediately usable to the people who needed to act on them. From these focused insights, we designed a detailed, week-by-week implementation plan for the transformation, including a "New Beginning" program with a unified vision, targeted communication upgrades (including a Management in Residence program), and change management events to align the entire sales organization behind a single direction.
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] The most useful thing about an independent diagnostic in a stalled integration is not the report — it is the permission it gives leadership to act on what they suspected but could not name. In this engagement, the "oil and water" description did not come from leadership: it came from the people living the integration on both sides. Surfacing that, naming it, and handing leadership a week-by-week plan — not a set of recommendations — removed the ambiguity that had kept the first integration effort paralysed. The findings were deliberately structured to be immediately usable to regional and channel leaders, because actionability, not insight, was the constraint.
type_of_reinvention: Post-acquisition integration, salesforce assessment and transformation design

source_file: 95 Consulting - Post Acquisition Talent Assessment - Food Manufacturing, China.pptx
hook: Ensuring the right people led a major food-industry acquisition — by assessing talent on both sides before integration decisions were made
challenges: Two incompatible corporate cultures — central control versus local autonomy — meant talent assessment had to evaluate cultural adaptability, not just role competence
High-potential future leaders in both organizations were not yet visible to regional leadership, and at risk of being lost in the transition without intervention
Tight global integration timetables created significant time pressure to design, field, and complete assessments across all key Asia markets before appointments locked in
ambition: A NYSE-listed food major with more than $35 billion in revenue was acquiring a global competitor in one of its key operating divisions. With both deal execution and integration timelines running simultaneously, the client recognized that its most consequential and least reversible decisions were not structural — they were human: who, from across two distinct talent pools, should lead the combined entity? The ambition was to answer that question with evidence rather than assumption, before integration appointments were locked in.
challenge: The two companies had built entirely different corporate cultures — one centered on central control and process discipline, the other on local autonomy and empowerment — making a straightforward capability assessment insufficient. Each candidate needed to be evaluated not just against their current role but against their ability to perform inside a combined entity that would demand a different kind of leadership from either organization. The additional risk was equally important: acquisitions routinely lose their best people in the transition, because high-potential individuals — not yet at senior levels — are invisible to the leadership making retention decisions. The assessment needed to find and surface them before integration reached the point of no return.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] We designed and fielded an independent talent assessment across both the acquirer and target organizations in all key Asia markets, working against the client's global integration timeline. Approximately 60 detailed assessment interviews were conducted across both organizations, with each individual evaluated against two parallel dimensions: professional capability and cultural adaptability — the ability to perform effectively inside the combined entity. Particular focus was placed on identifying high-potential future leaders: individuals not yet in senior enough roles to be visible to regional leadership, but with the profile to grow into them. Findings were structured to give regional leadership immediate, actionable guidance on integration appointments and, critically, on which individuals from both sides required active retention and development plans to avoid being lost in the transition.
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] The conventional risk in a post-acquisition talent assessment is getting the appointments wrong. The less obvious risk — and the one this engagement was explicitly designed to address — is getting the retention wrong. A common pattern in acquisitive companies is that the wrong people leave and the wrong people are retained, simply because talent in the middle of an organization is not visible to the leadership making decisions at the top. In this engagement, making future leaders visible before integration appointments were confirmed turned a structural risk into a structured priority. The appointments that followed were grounded in evidence, not hierarchy.
type_of_reinvention: Post-acquisition talent assessment and leadership identification

ambition: A rapidly growing Chinese integrated circuits manufacturer was approaching a foreign stock exchange listing without fully understanding the complexity it entailed. The PE group behind the company had no US listing experience either. What the company needed was a CFO who had navigated exactly this journey — someone who could take the company through a foreign listing from the inside, communicate credibly with Wall Street, and integrate into a management team that had not changed in a quarter of a century.
challenge: The candidate profile was unusually constrained: the CFO needed to have direct experience of taking a Chinese or Taiwanese company to a foreign listing — not adjacent experience, but proven precedent. They needed the cultural fluency to work inside a Chinese management team while projecting the credibility and communication style that institutional investors expected. And they needed to be someone the existing management — unchanged for 25 years — could accept and work alongside, in an organization that had no frame of reference for integrating a high-calibre external hire of this kind.
solutions: [DRAFT — for review] Rather than beginning with a market search, we first mapped the universe: we identified the CFOs of every Chinese and Taiwanese company that had listed on a foreign stock exchange, along with their immediate subordinates — extending the scan to Japan. This gave us the fullest possible population of candidates with direct, precedent experience of the exact journey the client was about to undertake. Within this population, we assessed each candidate for cultural fit — the ability to operate inside the company's management culture while communicating effectively with Wall Street — using in-depth interviews to surface individuals who combined the technical credentials with the interpersonal capability the role demanded. In parallel, we worked with the existing management team to prepare them for the cultural adjustment of welcoming a high-calibre external hire into a team that had operated without any outside appointments for 25 years.
client_insight: [DRAFT — for review] The most efficient way to find someone who has done something difficult before is to map everyone who has done it and then select. In a market as specialized as pre-IPO CFO placement for a Chinese company listing offshore, the universe of genuinely qualified candidates was not large — but it was mappable. Starting from the full precedent population, rather than from a candidate pipeline, meant that when the right person was identified, the confidence in the appointment was grounded in evidence. The cultural integration work with the existing management was the less visible but equally important piece: an appointment the organization cannot absorb is not a successful appointment.
